verdict: Conditionally sound, not yet execution-ready. The draft does preserve all requested feature families on paper while keeping Hermes primary, but the recommended Option 1 is still too generic at its center. I would approve the Hermes-core direction only after the plan is tightened around explicit event semantics, one write path per concept, a concrete mission schema, a single browser-terminal transport, and a hard boundary around model mutations.
strengths: The capability mapping is concrete: every external repo contributes named feature families with specific Hermes landing zones instead of being waved into a generic 'platform' bucket.

The recommendation fits observed seams in the codebase: plugin hooks already exist in `hermes_cli/plugins.py`, durable state already centers on `SessionDB` in `hermes_state.py`, `MemoryManager` already enforces the one-external-provider rule, `model_switch.py` already centralizes CLI+gateway model changes, and the TUI already runs through `tui_gateway`.
The non-goals cut off the most dangerous failure modes up front: no peer runtimes, no second canonical store, no legacy plugin-drop compatibility, and no silent model/env rewrites.
Risk posture is appropriate for the domain: monitor/propose/manual-approval rollout gates align with the existing approval and guard patterns rather than assuming day-one autonomy.
The projection language is mostly right: mirrors and cloud wrappers are treated as rebuildable outputs around Hermes rather than alternate truths.
steelman_antithesis: The strongest case against Option 1 is that it front-loads a platform rewrite around two generic primitives Hermes does not yet need: a new internal event bus and a much broader append-heavy SQLite control plane. Hermes already has public hook dispatch (`pre/post_tool_call`, `pre/post_llm_call`), a centralized approval system, a shared model-switch pipeline, and a single session store whose own comments explicitly discuss WAL contention and TUI freezes under multi-process write pressure. If the plan now routes tool/LLM/API/session/model traffic through a new generic bus and persists it into the same authority, it risks duplicating the hook system, promising stronger semantics than it can actually deliver, and creating a vague umbrella where future subsystems bypass the bus for performance. A service-first cutover over existing seams—typed mission, approval, proposal, projection, and model-op services with only selected durable audit records—could preserve the same feature families with less conceptual duplication and lower operational risk.
tradeoff_tensions: Canonical state vs projection ergonomics: one-way mirrors prevent split-brain, but they also mean FABRIC/Obsidian outputs cannot be treated as user-editable working state unless an import path is explicitly added later.
Reuse `tui_gateway` vs build a browser-native PTY service: reuse preserves one session/approval engine, while a dedicated browser transport fits cloud use better but risks a second runtime path.

Typed mission tree vs specialized tables: a unified `mission_nodes` model keeps cross-type queries and projections coherent, while specialized tables give tighter invariants but fragment the model and increase join complexity.

Full runtime event journal vs durable domain records: journaling everything improves auditability and learning, but the current SQLite design is already tuned around lock contention, so high-volume event persistence may directly fight runtime responsiveness.

Centralized model-ops authority vs operational convenience: forcing every model mutation through an approval/audit service improves safety and rollback, but leaving generic `/api/env` and config writes available is simpler in the short term and dangerous in the long term.

Core shared-memory service vs plugin/provider symmetry: making shared memory a first-class Hermes service respects the one-external-provider constraint, but it also creates a special case unless its boundary with `MemoryManager` is stated precisely.
principle_violations: Flag: false-contract risk. The draft's verification plan asks for 'exactly-once guarantees' for the event bus, but the plan does not define a transactional outbox, recovery model, or idempotency boundary that would make that promise true.

Flag: one concept / one representation is not yet enforced for mission work. `todo` is described as a projection of persistent mission state, but the draft does not say whether `todo` remains writable, becomes write-through, or becomes read-only when a mission is active.

Flag: one writable authority is not yet enforced for model identity. The plan adds a model-ops service, but the observed web layer still exposes generic config/env mutation paths; without a cutover rule, model state has multiple mutation channels.

Flag: internal event bus and public hook system are currently parallel abstractions. Unless the plan names a canonical event schema and translation direction, future contributors will not know which interface is authoritative.
synthesis: Keep the Hermes-core recommendation, but narrow it from 'generic event bus + generic control plane' into a service-first cutover. Use the existing hook/approval/model-switch/session seams as the ingress points. Define a small typed runtime-signal envelope for cross-surface coordination, but persist only business-significant records: missions, mission nodes, handoff packets, approvals, proposals, model mutations, projection cursors, and a bounded audit log. Make `todo` a write-through mission view when a mission is attached and keep it session-local otherwise. Make the browser terminal a thin server-side bridge onto the existing gateway session engine rather than a second runner. Make model changes execute only through a model-ops service that wraps the existing `model_switch.py` path and records approval plus rollback. That preserves all feature families while keeping Hermes primary and avoiding a vague umbrella core.
required_changes: Replace the phrase 'core-owned event bus' with an explicit contract: which existing call sites publish, what the canonical event envelope is, what ordering/dedup semantics are promised, and whether plugin hooks derive from signals or signals derive from hook invocations.
Remove or restate 'exactly-once' from the verification strategy unless the plan adds a transactional outbox/crash-recovery design; otherwise specify at-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers.

Lock the mission schema now. Recommend `missions` plus typed `mission_nodes`/`mission_links`, with separate tables for artifacts that have distinct lifecycles (`handoff_packets`, checkpoints, approvals, proposals, model mutations). Do not leave mission normalization to implementation time.
Specify `todo` semantics under an active mission: either it is a write-through facade over mission nodes or it becomes a read-only/session-local view. No dual writable representations.
Split control-plane persistence into authoritative domain state vs bounded audit/analytics records, and add a benchmark gate before enabling verbose runtime-event persistence in SQLite.
Resolve browser-terminal transport before Phase 5. Pick one canonical session engine and auth boundary; the cleanest fit is a server-side bridge to `tui_gateway`, not a second PTY/runtime stack.

Define a model-ops cutover rule: all model mutations must flow through a model-ops service that wraps `model_switch.py`, and generic `/api/env` or config updates must not be an untracked bypass for model-related keys.
State mirror semantics explicitly: Obsidian/FABRIC outputs are one-way, delete-and-rebuild projections with cursor/idempotency rules; direct edits are out of scope for the initial integration.
optional_improvements: Add a compact ownership diagram showing which module owns writes for policy, mission, proposal, projection, and model-op state.
Add explicit retention and sampling knobs for audit/event tables so operators can bound `state.db` growth before cloud/browser rollout.
Promote the already-existing approval queue and token-auth patterns as baseline implementation primitives in the plan, so Phase 5 does not reinvent auth and approval plumbing.
Attach quantitative exit criteria to the rollout gates: acceptable WAL lock budget, max concurrent sessions, and latency targets for dashboard/browser mission-control flows.
